title: Sociology
intro: Sociology questions are dominated by the classical European canon and by twentieth-century American theory in roughly equal measure. Max Weber, Erving Goffman, and Emile Durkheim are the three most-asked answers, followed closely by Pierre Bourdieu, Talcott Parsons, Georg Simmel, and C. Wright Mills. The classical triumvirate of Durkheim, Weber, and Marx anchors a cluster of foundational concepts — anomie, social facts, division of labor, bureaucracy, and the Protestant work ethic — while Goffman's dramaturgical theory and George Herbert Mead's symbolic interactionism supply the discipline's dominant micro-sociological vocabulary of roles, symbols, and the presentation of self.

A second major cluster concerns cities and urban life, running from Georg Simmel's essay on the metropolis through the Chicago School centered at the University of Chicago to Jane Jacobs's street-level critique of urban planning and the Broken Windows theory of crime and disorder. Later urban theorists such as David Harvey and Henri Lefebvre extend this tradition into a Marxist analysis of space, while community studies like Middletown and The Philadelphia Negro anchor it in specific American places. A parallel cluster addresses race, class, and gender inequality, drawing on W. E. B. Du Bois, Patricia Hill Collins, bell hooks, Judith Butler, and Angela Davis alongside concepts like intersectionality, patriarchy, and meritocracy.

Mid-century American structural functionalism, represented by Talcott Parsons and Robert K. Merton, contributes strain theory and middle-range theory as recurring answers, while a distinct conflict and critical-theory cluster spans C. Wright Mills's power elite, the Frankfurt School's Jurgen Habermas, and Michel Foucault's writing on power, knowledge, and prisons. Bourdieu's concepts of capital, habitus, and social capital connect to a separate network-theory cluster including weak ties, social networks, and Robert Putnam's civic-engagement research.

A smaller but consistent set of questions covers nations and global systems — Benedict Anderson's imagined communities, Immanuel Wallerstein's world-systems theory, and Charles Tilly's historical sociology of state formation and revolution — and the sociology of culture and media, including Marshall McLuhan, Daniel Bell's post-industrial society, and George Ritzer's McDonaldization thesis. Named works recur across these clusters as answers in their own right, among them The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life, The Sociological Imagination, Imagined Communities, and The Feminine Mystique.

sections.name: Classical Sociology
sections.blurb: The discipline's 19th- and early-20th-century founders and the core concepts they coined to explain modern social order.

entries.note: German sociologist of Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism; theorized bureaucracy, rationalization, and three types of authority.
French founder of sociology; wrote The Division of Labor in Society and Suicide; coined anomie and social facts.
German formal sociologist of social interaction and money; wrote "The Metropolis and Mental Life" and The Philosophy of Money.
German philosopher-economist (b. 1818) of historical materialism and class conflict; co-wrote The Communist Manifesto with Engels.
American economist-sociologist who coined "conspicuous consumption" in The Theory of the Leisure Class.
French sociologist and Durkheim's nephew; wrote The Gift, analyzing reciprocity and exchange in archaic societies.
Durkheim's concept of normlessness arising from breakdown of social regulation, linked to his study of suicide.
French founder who coined the term "sociology" and proposed positivism and the law of three stages.
Subject of Durkheim's landmark study distinguishing egoistic, altruistic, and anomic types by social integration.
French thinker who wrote Democracy in America, analyzing civic associations and the tyranny of the majority.
Studied in classical collective-behavior theory examining how individuals act differently in mass gatherings.
Analyzed by Tocqueville's Democracy in America as sustained by civic associations and equality of conditions.
Subject of Durkheim's The Division of Labor in Society, contrasting mechanical and organic solidarity.
British writer often called sociology's first female founder; translated Comte and wrote Society in America.
Analyzed by Durkheim's Elementary Forms of Religious Life and Weber's Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism.
Weber's model of rational-legal authority, characterized by hierarchy, rules, and impersonal administration.
Durkheim's term for external, coercive social phenomena like law and morality that constrain individual behavior.
Marx's collaborator who wrote The Condition of the Working Class in England and co-authored the Communist Manifesto.
Durkheim's term for cohesion in complex societies based on interdependence from specialized division of labor.
Compared with Protestants in Durkheim's Suicide, which found lower suicide rates linked to stronger social integration.

sections.name: Symbolic Interactionism & Micro-Sociology
sections.blurb: Micro-level approaches treating everyday interaction, role-play, and shared meaning as the basic unit of social life.

entries.note: Canadian-American sociologist of dramaturgical theory; wrote The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life and Stigma.
Micro-sociological tradition, rooted in George Herbert Mead, holding that meaning is created through social interaction.
American philosopher-sociologist who founded symbolic interactionism, theorizing the social self through "I" and "me."
Qualitative fieldwork method exemplified by Goffman's asylum studies and Laud Humphreys's Tearoom Trade.
American sociologist who founded ethnomethodology, studying how people produce shared social order through everyday practices.
Expected behaviors attached to social positions, central to Goffman's dramaturgical theory and role theory.
Common-link term spanning symbolic interactionism, Bourdieu's symbolic capital, and symbolic violence.
Shared meaningful signs that, per symbolic interactionism, are interpreted and negotiated through social interaction.

entries.topic: The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life
entries.answerline: The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life
entries.note: Erving Goffman's 1956 book applying dramaturgical theory, comparing social interaction to theatrical performance.
In Goffman's dramaturgical theory, individuals performing social roles before an audience in everyday interaction.

entries.topic: Tearoom Trade: Impersonal Sex in Public Places
entries.answerline: Tearoom Trade: Impersonal Sex in Public Places
entries.note: Laud Humphreys's controversial ethnography of anonymous encounters between men in public restrooms.

sections.name: Structural Functionalism & Middle-Range Theory
sections.blurb: Mid-20th-century American theory explaining social institutions by the functions they perform for the wider system.

entries.note: American sociologist who imported Weber to the US; wrote The Structure of Social Action; developed structural functionalism.
American sociologist who coined "self-fulfilling prophecy," Strain Theory, and middle-range theory.
Robert Merton's theory that deviance arises from a gap between culturally valued goals and legitimate means.
Robert Merton's approach bridging abstract grand theory and narrow empirical description.
Same sociologist as Robert King Merton, known for Strain Theory and middle-range theory.
Parsons-led paradigm analyzing society as an interconnected system of institutions serving functional needs.
Same sociologist as Robert King Merton, known for Strain Theory and middle-range theory.
Talcott Parsons's 1937 book synthesizing Weber, Durkheim, and Pareto into an action theory.

sections.name: Conflict, Power & Critical Theory
sections.blurb: Approaches centered on power, domination, and ideology, spanning Marxist-inflected conflict theory, the Frankfurt School, and Foucault.

entries.note: American sociologist who wrote The Power Elite and The Sociological Imagination, critiquing concentrated elite power.
German Frankfurt School theorist (b. 1929) of the public sphere and communicative action.
C. Wright Mills's concept of a unified elite spanning corporate, political, and military leadership.
Analyzed through Weber's monopoly of legitimate violence and Bourdieu's concept of symbolic violence.
Same sociologist as C. Wright Mills, author of The Power Elite and White Collar.
Antonio Gramsci's concept of ruling-class dominance maintained through cultural consent rather than force alone.
Jamaican-British cultural theorist who developed encoding/decoding media theory and studied hegemony and race.
French post-structuralist (b. 1926) of Discipline and Punish, theorizing power/knowledge and disciplinary institutions like prisons.
Political-economic ideology favoring markets and deregulation, critiqued by Harvey and other critical theorists.
Theory that power is dispersed among competing groups, opposed to Mills's power elite thesis.
Central concept analyzed by Weber, Foucault, Mills, and pluralist theorists as domination or dispersed capacity.
Institutions analyzed in Foucault's Discipline and Punish and criticized by Angela Davis's abolitionist writings.
C. Wright Mills's 1959 book urging the linkage of personal troubles to broad public issues.
C. Wright Mills's term for the salaried middle class, analyzed in his book White Collar.

sections.name: Urban Sociology & the Chicago School
sections.blurb: The study of cities, neighborhoods, and urban life, anchored by the Chicago School's ethnographic tradition and its later critics.

entries.note: Central subject of urban sociology, from the Chicago School's ecological models to Jane Jacobs's street-level critiques.
Urbanist who wrote The Death and Life of Great American Cities, championing sidewalks and mixed-use neighborhoods.
Studied via Strain Theory, Broken Windows theory, and Chicago School ecological approaches to deviance.
Home of the Chicago School of urban sociology, centered at the University of Chicago's sociology department.
Rule-breaking behavior explained by Strain Theory, labeling theory, and Chicago School ecological approaches.
Wilson and Kelling's theory that visible disorder like broken windows invites further crime and decay.
Marxist geographer of the urban process, spatial fixes, and "the right to the city."
French Marxist theorist of The Production of Space and "the right to the city."
Focus of Jane Jacobs's argument that pedestrian street life produces urban safety and community.
Object of study for the Chicago School's ecological models and later critical urban theorists like Harvey and Lefebvre.
Studied by the Chicago School through assimilation and ethnic-enclave models in American cities.
American urbanist and historian who wrote The City in History, critiquing sprawling suburban development.
Robert and Helen Lynd's landmark community study of Muncie, Indiana, as a typical American town.
Residential form critiqued by Lewis Mumford and studied as a counterpoint to Jane Jacobs's dense cities.
Georg Simmel's essay on how urban life produces a blasé attitude and mental overstimulation.
American metropolis studied in urban sociology alongside Chicago as a site of density, inequality, and street life.
City studied in Du Bois's pioneering urban sociological survey, The Philadelphia Negro.
Scottish biologist-urbanist who pioneered regional planning and coined "conurbation."
Analyzed by Henri Lefebvre's The Production of Space as socially produced rather than a neutral container.
Home institution of the Chicago School, whose sociology department pioneered urban ethnography in the early 1900s.

sections.name: Race, Class & Gender
sections.blurb: Scholarship on racial, economic, and gender inequality, including foundational Black sociology and second- and third-wave feminist theory.

entries.note: Concept coined by Kimberle Crenshaw describing overlapping systems of race, gender, and class oppression.
Social category theorized by Judith Butler as performative; central to feminist sociology and studies of masculinity.
Pioneering Black sociologist who wrote The Souls of Black Folk and The Philadelphia Negro; coined "double consciousness."
Gendered identity studied through concepts like Raewyn Connell's "hegemonic masculinity."
Black feminist sociologist who wrote Black Feminist Thought, developing the matrix of domination framework.
Studied through structural inequality, William Julius Wilson's urban poverty research, and welfare-state analysis.
American writer-sociologist who wrote Nickel and Dimed, an undercover account of low-wage work in America.
Black feminist scholar (Gloria Watkins) who wrote Ain't I a Woman, theorizing intersecting race, class, and gender oppression.
Studied as a social institution shaped by gender roles, class, and changing family structures.
Social category central to Du Bois's, Wilson's, and intersectionality scholars' analyses of inequality.
American sociologist who coined "emotional labor" in The Managed Heart, studying feeling in service work.
Philosopher-theorist of gender performativity, arguing gender is constituted through repeated acts, as in Gender Trouble.
Black feminist scholar-activist who wrote Women, Race, and Class and championed prison abolition.
Concept of advancement by talent and effort, critiqued by sociologists studying persistent class and racial inequality.
System of male-dominated social structures central to feminist sociological theory.
Subject of feminist sociological debate over its relation to patriarchy, exploitation, and sexual freedom.
Movement between class positions, studied as a measure of equality of opportunity and structural inequality.
Betty Friedan's 1963 book on suburban housewives' dissatisfaction, sparking second-wave feminism.
Feminist movement from the 1990s emphasizing intersectionality, individual identity, and critique of earlier feminist waves.
American sociologist who wrote The Truly Disadvantaged, linking urban poverty to deindustrialization and social isolation.
Social class studied by Marx and Engels, and by later sociologists of labor and inequality.

sections.name: Social Capital & Networks
sections.blurb: Theories of social ties, trust, and the convertible resources embedded in networks and communities.

entries.note: French sociologist of habitus and cultural, social, and symbolic capital; wrote Distinction on taste and class.
Ties linking individuals or groups, studied via network analysis, weak-tie theory, and social capital research.
Resources embedded in social networks, theorized by Bourdieu and popularized by Robert Putnam's Bowling Alone.
Mark Granovetter's concept that loose acquaintances, not close ties, best transmit new information like job leads.
In Bourdieu's framework, includes economic, cultural, social, and symbolic forms convertible into social advantage.
Bourdieu's concept of internalized dispositions shaping perception and behavior, shaped by one's social position.
American political scientist who wrote Bowling Alone, arguing American social capital and civic engagement declined.
Structures of social ties analyzed through network analysis, weak-tie theory, and social capital research.

sections.name: Nations, States & Global Systems
sections.blurb: Macro-historical sociology of nation-states, nationalism, revolutions, and the modern global economy.

entries.note: American sociologist who developed world-systems theory, dividing the global economy into core, periphery, and semi-periphery.
National context for studies of civil society and social capital, as in Putnam's Making Democracy Work.
Political communities analyzed through Benedict Anderson's "imagined communities" and theories of nationalism.
Political scientist who wrote Imagined Communities, defining nations as socially constructed political communities.
American historical sociologist who studied state formation, revolutions, and contentious politics, coining "war made the state."
National context for sociological studies of state formation, revolution, and rapid modernization.
Nation studied in sociology as an early industrial society, from Engels's Manchester to class and welfare-state analysis.
Process of increasing worldwide economic and cultural interconnection, analyzed through Wallerstein's world-systems theory.
Benedict Anderson's 1983 book arguing nations are constructed through shared print media and imagination.
Ideology of national identity and self-determination, theorized through Anderson's imagined communities.
Studied alongside war and revolution in historical and political sociology, as in Charles Tilly's work.
National context for studies of nationalism, state socialism, and social movements like Solidarity.
Studied by historical sociologists like Charles Tilly, explaining state breakdown and collective political upheaval.

sections.name: Culture, Media & Modernity
sections.blurb: Theories of mass culture, media, and the changing character of the self and society under modern conditions.

entries.note: British sociologist of structuration theory and "runaway world" globalization; advised Tony Blair's Third Way.
German sociologist who wrote The Civilizing Process, tracing long-term shifts in manners and self-restraint.
Shared beliefs, symbols, and practices studied across sociology, from Bourdieu's cultural capital to cultural studies.
Austrian psychoanalyst whose theories of the unconscious and civilization influenced sociological theories of the self.
Mass medium analyzed in studies of media effects, consumer culture, and McLuhan's media theory.
American sociologist who wrote The Coming of Post-Industrial Society, forecasting a knowledge- and service-based economy.
Social institution studied for its role in socialization, credentialing, and the reproduction of inequality.
American sociologist who coined "McDonaldization," describing rationalized efficiency spreading through modern society.
Studied in the sociology of knowledge, examining how social context shapes what counts as truth.
Canadian media theorist who coined "the medium is the message" and "global village."
Condition of modern society theorized by Giddens and others as marked by rationalization and reflexivity.
Field, associated with E. O. Wilson, explaining social behavior through evolutionary biology, controversial among sociologists.
David Riesman's book distinguishing inner-directed and other-directed character types in modern American society.

appendix.answer: "The Master's Tools Will Never Dismantle the Master's House"
Bowling Alone: The Collapse and Revival of the American Community
Distinction: A Social Critique of the Judgment of Taste
Imagined Communities: Reflections on the Origin and Spread of Nationalism
Middletown: A Study in Modern American Culture
The Beauty Myth: How Images of Beauty Are Used Against Women
The Division of Labor in Society
The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism
White Collar: The American Middle Classes
